q: I am not free tomorrow morning. I ___ (have got) a driving lesson.
a: I am not free tomorrow morning. I've got a driving lesson.

q: Rachel is an only child. She ___ (have got) no brothers or sisters.
a: Rachel is an only child. She's got no brothers or sisters.

q: We ___ (have got) plenty of time. There is no need to hurry.
a: We've got plenty of time. There is no need to hurry.

q: You ___ (have got) a really good voice. I wish I could sing as well as you.
a: You've got a really good voice. I wish I could sing as well as you.

q: I don't feel very well this morning. I ___ (have got) a sore throat.
a: I don't feel very well this morning. I've got a sore throat.

q: Laura studied at university. She ___ (have got) a degree in physics.
a: Laura studied at university. She's got a degree in physics.

q: I ___ (have got) a question. Maybe you can answer it.
a: I've got a question. Maybe you can answer it.

q: James ___ (have got) a lot of experience. I think he should get the job.
a: James has got a lot of experience. I think he should get the job.

q: She couldn't get into the house. She ___ (not / have) a key.
a: She couldn't get into the house. She didn't have a key.

q: Is there anything you would like to ask? ___ (you / have) any questions?
a: Is there anything you would like to ask? Do you have any questions?

q: They can't pay their bills. They ___ (not / have) any money.
a: They can't pay their bills. They don't have any money.

q: We got wet in the rain yesterday. We ___ (not / have) an umbrella.
a: We got wet in the rain yesterday. We didn't have an umbrella.

q: Jack ___ (not / have) a car. He can't afford one and he can't drive anyway.
a: Jack doesn't have a car. He can't afford one and he can't drive anyway.

q: 'Excuse me, ___ (you / have) a pen I could borrow?' 'Yes, sure. Here you are.'
a: 'Excuse me, do you have a pen I could borrow?' 'Yes, sure. Here you are.'

q: I was very busy yesterday. I ___ (not / have) time to go shopping.
a: I was very busy yesterday. I didn't have time to go shopping.

q: 'Tell me about Jack. ___ (he / have) a job?' 'Yes, he works at the hospital.'
a: 'Tell me about Jack. Does he have a job?' 'Yes, he works at the hospital.'

q: When you worked in your last job, ___ (you / have) your own office?
a: When you worked in your last job, did you have your own office?

q: 'Where is the remote control?' 'I don't know. I ___ (not / have) it.'
a: 'Where is the remote control?' 'I don't know. I don't have it.'

q: 'Tom ___ (have) a motorbike, didn't he?' 'Yes, that's right. A long time ago.'
a: 'Tom had a motorbike, didn't he?' 'Yes, that's right. A long time ago.'

q: Correct the grammar error: 'Lisa had got long hair when she was a child.'
a: Lisa had long hair when she was a child.

q: Correct the grammar error: 'I couldn't contact you because I hadn't my phone.'
a: I couldn't contact you because I didn't have my phone.

q: Correct the grammar error: 'Are you feeling OK?' 'No, I'm having a cold.'
a: 'Are you feeling OK?' 'No, I have a cold.'

q: Correct the grammar error: 'I felt really tired. I hadn't any energy.'
a: I felt really tired. I didn't have any energy.

q: Correct the grammar error: 'Was your trip OK? Had you any problems?'
a: Was your trip OK? Did you have any problems?

q: Correct the grammar error: 'The last time I saw Steve, he was having a beard.'
a: The last time I saw Steve, he had a beard.

q: Correct the grammar error: 'How often have you a shower?'
a: How often do you have a shower?

q: I don't eat much during the day. I never ___ (have / lunch).
a: I don't eat much during the day. I never have lunch.

q: David starts work at 8 o'clock and ___ (have / a break) at 10.30.
a: David starts work at 8 o'clock and has a break at 10.30.

q: We ___ (have / a party) last week. We invited lots of people.
a: We had a party last week. We invited lots of people.

q: There's something wrong with my bike. Can you ___ (have / a look) at it for me?
a: There's something wrong with my bike. Can you have a look at it for me?

q: Joe is away on holiday at the moment. I hope he ___ (have / a nice time).
a: Joe is away on holiday at the moment. I hope he is having a nice time.

q: I met some friends in the supermarket yesterday. We stopped and ___ (have / a chat).
a: I met some friends in the supermarket yesterday. We stopped and had a chat.

q: ___ (you / have / trouble) finding the book you wanted? 'No, I found it OK.'
a: Did you have trouble finding the book you wanted? 'No, I found it OK.'

q: Suzanne ___ (have / a baby) a few weeks ago. It's her second child.
a: Suzanne had a baby a few weeks ago. It's her second child.

q: I ___ (have / a shower) when the light went out suddenly.
a: I was having a shower when the light went out suddenly.

q: I'd like to go away somewhere. I ___ (not / have / a holiday) for a long time.
a: I'd like to go away somewhere. I haven't had a holiday for a long time.
